latest.title: Full Year Trading Update
latest.content:
20 May 2026 07:00
RNS Number : 9780E
Knights Group Holdings PLC
20 May 2026
Knights Group Holdings plc
("Knights", the "Company" or the "Group")
Full Year Trading Update
Strong revenue and profit performance, with double-digit organic growth in the second half
Knights, the national legal and professional services business, today issues a trading update for the year ended 30 April 2026 which anticipates:
A 28% increase in full year revenue to c.£207m (FY25: £162m).
​
Double digit organic growth in the second half (rising from 2.6% in the first half) meaning the Group achieved organic growth in line with its expectations for the full year.Underlying EBITDA¹ to increase by 19% to c.£51m (FY25: £43m) with Underlying PBT¹ increasing by 18% to c.£33m (FY25: £28m).
The Group maintained its disciplined approach to working capital management throughout the year, with debtor days² of 30 at 30 April 2026 (FY25: 31 days), supporting strong underlying cash conversion. The Group maintained a robust balance sheet position, with net debt³ expected to be c.£65.4m at year end (FY25: £64.8m), after c.£17m of net cash paid in relation to acquisitions.
Knights continues to attract high calibre professionals through its national scale, a growing reputation, its market-leading resources, location network and expertise in the UK regions; alongside its unique collaborative culture. Annualised churn
4
has remained low.
In line with its value-accretive acquisition strategy, Knights completed the acquisition of Birkett Long LLP and Birkett Long IFA LLP in June 2025 and Rix & Kay LLP in August 2025, further strengthening its presence in the South East. Having established Knights' first presence outside England, in Cardiff, organically, we added to the team via the acquisition of Le Gros Solicitors Limited in August 2025. These and prior year acquisitions have all performed well.
The Group is actively assessing acquisition opportunities, with a healthy pipeline. We continue to progress the previously announced discussions with Moore Barlow LLP.
David Beech, CEO of Knights, said:
"Knights has delivered a strong financial performance in FY26, driven by organic growth, our successful acquisition strategy, and the sustained benefits of our differentiated business model, all underpinned by disciplined execution led by our expanded management team.''
Knights expects to announce its full year results on 6 July 2026.
Notes
1 Underlying EBITDA excludes non-underlying revenue and operating expenses including transaction and onerous lease expenses in relation to acquisitions, contingent acquisition payments, disposal of acquired assets, along with one-off restructuring and professional expenses, mainly incurred on acquisitions, through streamlining support functions or strategic reorganisations.
Underlying PBT also excludes amortisation of acquired intangibles, non-underlying finance costs and a gain on disposal of a customer list from an acquired business.
2
Debtor days are calculated on a count back basis using the gross debtors at the period end and compared with total fees raised over prior months.
3
Net debt includes cash and cash equivalents, borrowings and acquired debt but excludes lease liabilities.
4 Churn is calculated based on the number of qualified fee earners who had been employed by the Group for more than one year. Churn is calculated taking the number of leavers in the above group over the financial year as a percentage of the average number of colleagues for the year. Churn excludes expected churn from acquisitions in the year of acquisition and the first full year post acquisition, redundancies and retirements. Retention is 100% less the churn rate.
Enquiries
Knights
David Beech, CEO
Kate Lewis, CFO
James Sheridan, Chief M&A Officer / Investor Relations
Via MHP
Deutsche Numis (Nomad and Broker)
Stuart Skinner, Kevin Cruickshank
+44 20 7260 1000
MHP (Media enquiries)
Katie Hunt, Eleni Menikou, Lucy Gibbs
+44 (0) 7884 494112
knights@mhpgroup.com
Notes to Editors
Knights is a fast-growing, legal and professional services business, ranked within the UK's top 50 largest law firms by revenue. Knights was one of the first law firms in the UK to move from the traditional partnership model to a corporate structure in 2012 and has since grown rapidly. Knights has specialists in all key areas of Corporate and Commercial law and Private Wealth services. It is focussed on key UK markets outside London and currently operates from 29 offices located in Beaconsfield, Birmingham, Brighton, Bristol, Cardiff, Carlisle, Chelmsford, Cheltenham, Chester, Colchester, Exeter, Kings Hill, Leeds, Leicester, Lincoln, Manchester, Newcastle, Nottingham, Oxford, Portsmouth, Reading, Sheffield, Stoke, Teesside, Uxbridge, Weybridge, Wilmslow, Worcester and York.

latest.title: Response to Press Speculation
latest.content:
25 Mar 2026 12:41
RNS Number : 1189Y
Knights Group Holdings PLC
25 March 2026
Knights Group Holdings plc
("Knights", the "Company" or the "Group")
Response to Press Speculation
Knights, the legal and professional services business, notes the recent press speculation regarding a potential acquisition by the Group of Moore Barlow LLP.
Knights' strategy is to build the leading premium, fully collaborative legal and professional services business across the UK regions. As part of this strategy, it regularly considers selected value-accretive strategic opportunities with a strong cultural fit and the potential to support future organic growth.
Knights maintains regular dialogue with a number of law firms and confirms that it is currently in discussions with Moore Barlow LLP regarding a potential acquisition. However, there can be no certainty that any transaction will be agreed, nor as to the terms of any such transaction.
A further announcement will be made if and when appropriate.
Enquiries
Knights
David Beech, CEO
James Sheridan, M&A/Investor Relations
Via MHP
Deutsche Bank Numis (Nomad and Broker)
Stuart Skinner, Kevin Cruickshank
020 7260 1000

Katie Hunt, Eleni Menikou, Lucy Gibbs
+44 (0) 7884 494112knights@mhpgroup.com
About Knights
Knights is a fast-growing, legal and professional services business, ranked within the UK's top 50 largest law firms by revenue. Knights was one of the first law firms in the UK to move from the traditional partnership model to a corporate structure in 2012 and has since grown rapidly. Knights has specialists in all key areas of Corporate and Commercial law and Private Wealth services. It is focussed on key UK markets outside London and currently operates from 29 offices located in Beaconsfield, Birmingham, Brighton, Bristol, Cardiff, Carlisle, Chelmsford, Cheltenham, Chester, Colchester, Exeter, Kings Hill, Leeds, Leicester, Lincoln, Manchester, Newcastle, Nottingham, Oxford, Portsmouth, Reading, Sheffield, Stoke, Teesside, Uxbridge, Weybridge, Wilmslow, Worcester and York.

company_30s.what_they_do: KGH Ltd. is a UK‑listed investment holding that builds and manages a portfolio of mid‑market businesses across a handful of sectors.  It looks for growth opportunities, acquires companies, and then supports them with capital, strategy and operational expertise.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The firm sits on a niche of UK companies that are too small for big private‑equity players but too large for start‑ups.  By adding value to these businesses it can generate attractive risk‑adjusted returns for its shareholders while keeping a diversified exposure to the broader UK economy.
company_30s.current_state: KGH has recently completed a block listing and used the proceeds to acquire Birkett Long, a property specialist, signalling a shift toward a more tangible asset base.  The company is consolidating its holdings and waiting for the next round of capital deployment.  It is trading well below its historical highs, suggesting a significant price pullback.

rns_narrative: KGH’s latest filings show a tidy mix of governance updates (director shareholding, nominated adviser change), a full‑year results release that confirms steady cash generation, and a strategic move – the acquisition of Birkett Long – that adds a steady‑income property arm to its portfolio.

These events paint a picture of a company that is actively stabilising after a period of volatility: it is tightening governance, reinforcing its balance sheet, and expanding into a new, lower‑risk asset class.
If the market recognises the value of the new property holdings and the company’s improved cash flow, the stock could start to find a new floor.

crowd_tape.dominant_theme: Quiet optimism – a handful of bullish voices, no real opposition.
crowd_tape.bull_narrative: The small group of bullish posts praise KGH’s solid results, its cash‑rich stance, and the potential upside from the Birkett Long acquisition.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: There are no clear bearish narratives; the only negative voice mentions unrelated industry concerns and does not target KGH directly.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The sentiment has stayed steady and bullish, but the chatter remains low, hinting that the market has not yet fully priced in the recent moves.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends for the ticker is dead – no public search interest, which means the market is still unaware of the latest developments.

near_term_catalysts: The next quarterly earnings release (expected in early Q3) will test the company’s cash‑flow narrative and could provide a clear sign of whether the Birkett Long acquisition is delivering the expected returns.
Any announcement of new capital raising or a strategic partnership to grow the property side would lift investor confidence.
A noticeable uptick in trading volume around the company’s key support levels (the 50‑day moving average) would signal that buyers are re‑entering the market.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a sustained close above the 50‑day moving average, a spike in volume that outpaces the 30‑day average, and a positive earnings surprise.  If the company misses earnings or shows a decline in cash generation, the upside narrative weakens.
expect_next.timing_regime: The price appears to be in the early stages of a recovery from a deep pullback.  A clear bounce would need confirmation of support and a rebound in volume; once those are in place, the mean‑reversion thesis becomes credible.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: KGH’s recent deep price drop (over 70% from its peak) sets up a classic asymmetric play.  If the stock recovers to the midpoint of its previous range, the upside is roughly double the current level – a 100% gain.  The acquisition of Birkett Long adds a steady‑income layer that can cushion the company’s earnings, making a rebound more likely.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: The main risk is that the new property asset may take longer than expected to generate returns, or that the company’s earnings miss expectations, which would keep the stock languishing.  A realistic downside is a 10–15% further drop if the market stays skeptical.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Potential upside: 100% (doubling).  Potential downside: 10–15%.  Risk‑reward ratio: roughly 7:1 if the price recovers to the midpoint, which is highly attractive for a contrarian play.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter when the price closes above the 50‑day moving average and volume is at least 20% higher than the 30‑day average.  Allocate 5–10% of the portfolio to the position, with a stop 10% below the entry to protect against a false breakout.
playbook.if_watching: Wait for a clear earnings beat or a press release that confirms the property acquisition is generating cash.  Also keep an eye on any sign of new capital raising – that would lift the price further.
playbook.if_passing: If the company announces a significant liquidity crisis or a regulatory penalty that threatens its operations, consider exiting.  Otherwise, the deep crash and lack of terminal risk keep the bullish stance intact.

ai_insights.bottom_line: KGH has plunged past 70% of its peak, is quietly gaining traction with a new property acquisition, and has a clear path to a 100% upside if earnings and volume confirm a bounce – a high‑reward, low‑downside contrarian play.
